expectations.text: States the fundamental difference correctly: AI RMF is voluntary risk guidance; the EU AI Act is binding law with penalties, conformity assessment, and CE marking — RMF alignment alone never satisfies the AI Act
expectations.evidence: "AI RMF is a voluntary, flexible framework; the EU AI Act is binding law with specific mandatory obligations, deadlines, and penalties. This distinction drives most of the gaps." CE marking and conformity assessment covered explicitly as a no-RMF-equivalent gap.

expectations.text: Provides sensible construct mappings: GOVERN ↔ QMS Art. 17 / accountability; MAP ↔ risk classification + intended-purpose documentation; MEASURE ↔ Art. 15 accuracy/robustness testing + Art. 9 risk management evidence; MANAGE ↔ Art. 9 lifecycle risk management + post-market monitoring Art. 72
expectations.evidence: "GOVERN | Risk management system (Art. 9), quality management system (Art. 17)..."; "MAP | Risk categorization (prohibited/high-risk/limited-risk/minimal-risk tiers under Art. 5-6, Annex III), intended purpose documentation"; MEASURE mapped to 'conformity assessment (Art. 43)... accuracy/robustness/cybersecurity requirements (Art. 15)'; MANAGE mapped to 'Post-market monitoring (Art. 72), incident reporting (Art. 73).' Matches the assertion's suggested anchors closely, including the explicit Art. 17 QMS reference for GOVERN.

expectations.text: Identifies concrete AI Act obligations with no RMF equivalent: conformity assessment/CE marking, EU database registration, technical documentation per Annex IV, serious-incident reporting, FRIA (Art. 27) where applicable, prohibited-practice screening
expectations.evidence: Numbered gap list: legal risk classification (item 1), conformity assessment/CE marking/EU database Art. 71 (item 2), FRIA Art. 27 (item 3), Annex IV technical documentation (item 4), mandatory incident reporting deadlines (item 5), prohibited practices screening Art. 5 (item 7) — all explicitly covered.

expectations.text: Notes risk-tiering difference: AI Act's fixed legal tiers (prohibited/high/limited/minimal + GPAI) vs RMF's contextual risk assessment
expectations.evidence: "prohibited (Art. 5)... high-risk (Art. 6/Annex III)... limited risk... or minimal risk. Gap: AI RMF doesn't have a legally defined risk taxonomy... AI RMF leaves risk categorization to organizational judgment." GPAI obligations covered separately as item 8.

expectations.text: Recommends a practical integration: use RMF processes as the management-system engine, then overlay AI Act artifact requirements — one programme, two outputs
expectations.evidence: "Use AI RMF as your overarching risk management operating model... and layer the EU AI Act's specific legal requirements as a compliance checklist on top of it... Many organizations build a crosswalk matrix mapping each AI RMF subcategory to specific AI Act articles... to avoid duplicating effort."
